Comment: Header for the Keystone Nature need group in the wellbeing panel.
Nature need flavor — filled by visiting a contemplation building surrounded by Forest biome.
Nature need flavor — filled by visiting a contemplation building surrounded by Grassland biome.
Nature need flavor — filled by visiting a contemplation building surrounded by Wetland biome (the Lido is the only vanilla source today).
Tooltip on the bottom-bar group button.

Text: Force-places a Class B flourish appropriate to the clicked tile's biome -- bypasses the score/threshold gate the auto-spawn handler uses. Picks the biome with the highest score at the cursor (among biomes that have a registered Class B recipe) and spawns its first registered recipe. Useful for staging content without waiting for ecology dynamics to catch up.

Spawns a Class A decoration -- non-blocking, non-persisted, with a moisture-driven controller. Sample uses a Dandelion clone (alive when moist, dry when not). Reactivity is the orthogonal axis -- the same Class A bucket also hosts purely passive content.
Spawns a Class A decoration carrying a Unity ParticleSystem. Passive (no ecology reactivity, no interaction) -- demonstrates the same registry can host non-mesh decorations like atmospheric effects.

Spawns a Class D natural resource: vanilla flora from the OTHER faction (CoffeeBush in a Folktails game, Maple in IronTeeth). Plantable / Gatherable specs are stripped by Keystone's faction-strip patch so the active faction's UIs don't crash on it.
Spawns a Class D natural resource: a Pine, available natively in both factions. Full vanilla pipeline -- can be cut, planted, gathered (where applicable), and reacts to moisture/flood normally.

Spawns a Class A decoration cloning the Keystone-original Rock_medium_1 blueprint. End-to-end smoke test of the custom-mesh pipeline: Quaternius FBX -> Blender -> .timbermesh -> TimbermeshSpec -> KeystoneMaterialProvider resolves the KeystoneRock material -> rendered in-game. Retired once Class C rock spawning is wired through the recipe system.

Spawns a random KeystoneRockCluster* inanimate Class C blueprint at the cursor. No lifecycle (rocks don't wither); runtime biome-driven re-tint via KeystoneRockTint adjusts material to mossy / dry / default based on the tile's dominant biome. Bypasses the Class C recipe gate so the cluster lands regardless of biome+level conditions -- useful for verifying tint behaviour across biomes.

Comment: Shared display name for all KeystoneRockCluster* variants.

Shared display name for all Keystone flourish blueprints. Surfaced only by vanilla's loading-issue cleanup path when a stale flourish is deleted on load; flourishes are non-selectable so it isn't shown in normal play.

Text: Spawns a KeystoneDeer BlockObject at the cursor. End-to-end smoke test of the custom-mesh pipeline for fauna assets: Quaternius .blend -> Blender shade-smooth + VAT palette bake -> .timbermesh -> KeystoneDeer material -> rendered in-game. Hardcoded blueprint, no biome resolution. Replaced or generalised once proper fauna content infrastructure (Class E / agent system) lands.
Fish: Place KeystoneFish1 above water (smoke test)

Spawns a KeystoneFish1 static prop at the cursor and starts looping its Default animation clip. Gated on water depth: click a dry tile and nothing spawns; click a water tile and a fish appears just above the water surface, swimming in place. No agent, no pathfinding, no despawn — pure visual verification of the fish mesh + Default clip. Reload to clear accumulated fish.
Places a mature Birch, kills it, and removes its yield — leaving a harvested stump. Used to test stump-replacement logic in Class B/D spawn handlers.

Force-places the KeystoneDeadPine1 flourish — a vanilla dead-pine trunk wrapped in the Keystone PineIvy mesh — at the clicked tile, bypassing biome/score gates. Smoke test for the dead-tree + fitted-ivy composition.

Click a tree to cycle its overgrowth: barren -> overgrown -> reseeded (the tree is replaced by a new seedling that drops the felled wood and comes out overgrown). Validates the overgrowth overlay and the reseed mechanism by hand; bypasses the natural maturity/dead-tree gates.

Comment: Player-facing planting brush in the field/crop menu.

Text: Drag to mark an area for planting. Each tile is sown with a weighted-random pick from the crop types below — raise a type's weight to plant more of it, drop it to zero to leave it out — an easy way to lay out a varied field instead of one crop at a time. Crops are still planted by your farmers through the normal planting queue.

Comment: Header of the mixed-crop options panel.
Player-facing planting brush in the forester tree/bush menu.

Text: Drag to mark an area for planting. Each tile is sown with a weighted-random pick from the tree and bush types below — raise a type's weight to plant more of it, drop it to zero to leave it out — a quick way to grow a natural, mixed woodland. Everything is still planted by your foresters through the normal planting queue.

Comment: Header of the mixed-forest options panel.
Button that sets every species in the mixed-planting panel to weight 1 (an even mix).
Button that sets every species in the mixed-planting panel to weight 0 (none planted).
Row in the mix that weights how much bare ground the brush leaves: raise it for more open space, 0 for a fully-planted area.

When on, the brush also marks tiles that already hold a plant or tree (never buildings or paths); the new planting fills in once that tile is cleared. Off by default, matching the vanilla planting tool, which skips occupied tiles.

When on, any plant or tree on a brushed tile is destroyed — removed, not harvested for resources — so the new planting can take its place. Off by default: leave it off to queue plantings without killing what's already growing. Has no effect unless Overwrite is on.
Player-facing cutting-planner brush in the forester tree-cutting menu.

Text: Drag over an area to mark its trees for cutting — set the percentage below to choose how many: 100% clear-cuts the selected tree types, less thins them, marking roughly that fraction at random and leaving the rest standing. Re-drag the same area to roll a different mix. Trees are still felled by your foresters through the normal cutting queue.

Comment: Header of the cutting-planner options panel.
Slider for the share of eligible trees the brush marks for cutting; the live percentage shows to the right of the slider.

When on (the default), each drag first clears any cutting marks on the selected tree types in the area before re-marking, so the area is set to the chosen percentage rather than piling marks up across drags. Turn off to only add marks.
Header above the per-species toggles in the logging panel.
Button that includes every tree type in the logging selection.
Button that excludes every tree type from the logging selection.

Text: Note: this tool can be turned off in Mod Settings → Keystone (UI section).
Comment: Final line of each Keystone tool button description; points players to the on/off toggle.
Header above the flora section of the Keystone settings panel.
Class B flourish density slider label.

Text:
How many small plants — flowers, ferns, cattails, and the like — appear in each biome.

0% removes them entirely.
100% is the default amount.
200% doubles the amount.

These plants are most of what makes Keystone visible on the map — at 0% the ecology still runs, but you won't see much of it.

Lower this if your computer struggles on foliage-heavy maps.

Comment: Class D Trees density slider label.

Text:
How many wild trees (birch, maple, oak, chestnut, mangrove) appear as part of each biome.

0% removes them entirely.
100% is the default amount.
200% doubles the amount.

Lower this if your map feels too crowded with trees, or if you want more building space.

Comment: Class D Bushes density slider label.

Text:
How many wild bushes (blueberry, dandelion) appear as part of each biome.

Bushes are planted by the forester and persist after harvest.

0% removes them entirely.
100% is the default amount.
200% doubles the amount.

Comment: Class D Crops density slider label.

Text:
How many wild aquatic crops (cattail, spadderdock) appear as part of each biome.

Crops are planted by the farmer and harvested as the whole plant.

0% removes them entirely.
100% is the default amount.
200% doubles the amount.

Comment: Warmup slider label.

Text:
NEW GAMES ONLY — this setting has no effect on loaded saves.

Simulates this many days of ecosystem development when starting a new settlement.

0 = start from scratch — biomes build up from nothing over the first few game-days.
3 = the default — basic biomes are established and visible from the start.
7 = mature ecosystem — higher-level biomes are well underway from day one.

Comment: Growth bonus slider label.

Text:
Maximum growth-speed bonus for plants in a healthy biome. Trees grow faster in forests, water plants in wetlands.

0% disables the bonus entirely.
30% is the default (up to 30% faster growth at full biome health).
100% allows up to double growth speed.

Comment: Header above the fauna section of the Keystone settings panel.
Master fauna toggle label.

Text:
Master switch for all wildlife — deer, cattle, and fish.

Turning this off removes any currently-spawned animals and stops new ones from appearing.

The sliders below have no effect when this is off.

Comment: Per-species deer slider label.

Text:
How many deer appear in suitable areas.

0% removes deer entirely.
100% is the default amount.
200% doubles the population.

Comment: Per-species cattle slider label.

Text:
How many cows and bulls appear in suitable areas.

0% removes them entirely.
100% is the default amount.
200% doubles the population.

Comment: Per-species fish slider label.

Text:
How many fish appear in suitable water.

0% removes fish entirely.
100% is the default amount.
200% doubles the population.

Comment: Header above the dead-tree overgrowth section of the Keystone settings panel.
Overgrowth spawn-rate slider label.

Text:
How quickly trees get overgrown with weeds, bushes, and moss. Purely visual — it does not change how fast dead trees are replaced.

0% turns overgrowth visuals off entirely.
100% is the default rate.
200% is twice as fast.

Comment: Dead-tree replacement-rate slider label.

Text:
How quickly mature dead trees are replaced by new saplings — leaving wood on the ground to haul, like a felled tree. Works whether or not the tree is overgrown.

0% means dead trees are never replaced.
100% is the default speed.
200% is twice as fast.

Comment: Header above the atmospheric-effects section of the Keystone settings panel.
Foggy-morning frequency dropdown label.

Text:
How often a misty morning rolls in over wetland areas.

Off — no foggy mornings ever.
Rare — roughly once a week.
Sometimes — about one in four mornings (the default).
Often — about half of all mornings.
Always — every morning.

Only affects wetlands.

Comment: Dropdown option: no foggy mornings.
Dropdown option: foggy mornings roughly once a week.
Dropdown option: foggy mornings about one in four days.
Dropdown option: foggy mornings about half of all mornings.
Dropdown option: foggy morning every day.
Header above the performance-tuning section of the Keystone settings panel.
Map-update cadence slider label.

Text:
How often Keystone re-checks the state of the map (moisture, water, contamination) and updates which biomes are present where.

1 = every in-game hour (the default; biomes react most quickly to player changes).
4 = once every four hours (lighter on the CPU on large maps; biomes react more slowly).

Has no effect on wildlife — use the Fauna sliders to tune that.

Only changeable from the main menu.

Comment: Rules-applier cadence slider label.

Text:
How often Keystone re-evaluates which flourishes, decorations, and attrition rules should fire on each tile.

1 = once per in-game day (the default; flora and decor appear and are reclaimed most quickly).
4 = once every four in-game days (lightest on the CPU on large maps; flora and decor change more slowly).

This is the biggest CPU lever on saturated maps — most of the per-tile work is in spawning new objects, and slowing the cycle cuts it proportionally.

Only changeable from the main menu.

Comment: Header above the base-game-tweaks section of the Keystone settings panel.
Vanilla natural-reproduction throttle slider label.

Text:
How fast wild plants and trees spread to nearby tiles on their own — as a share of the base game's normal rate.

100% is the normal rate.
5% is the default — wild spread is slowed to a trickle.
0% stops wild spread entirely.

Keystone already fills each biome with its own plants and trees, so the base game's slow creep is mostly redundant — the low default lets Keystone drive what grows where, instead of two systems competing.

This is a slow-down only; it can't make wild plants spread faster than the base game.

Only changeable from the main menu.

Biome overlay opacity slider label.

Text: How opaque the biome overlay boxes appear when the overlay is active. 25% lets the terrain show through clearly, 100% is fully opaque. Default 75%.
